exemplar.title: What role do models play in the production of knowledge?

detailedExemplarData.title: What role do models play in the production of knowledge?

strengths: Clear identification of three specific real-world objects with authentic contexts
Explicit links made between each object and the IA prompt about models in knowledge production
Use of relevant TOK concepts (e.g. bias, uncertainty, revision) to justify object inclusion
Solid illustrative details (CRISPR, rational-actor assumption, Eurocentric bias) that ground the arguments
weaknesses: Analysis occasionally becomes descriptive or generic rather than sustained critical evaluation
Uneven evidential support and limited citations weaken academic rigor
Repetition across object justifications and imprecise phrasing in places reduce clarity
Inconsistent formatting in bibliography and figure captions detracts from polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exhibition meets the criteria for the 5â6 band: it identifies three well-contextualized objects, explains their relevance to the prompt, and offers mostly coherent justifications supported by TOK ideas and examples. However, uneven depth of analysis, occasional generic commentary, limited citation practice and minor clarity issues prevent it from achieving the higher band. Given these strengths and shortcomings, a mark of 6 accurately reflects its solid but not exemplary performance.

annotations.comment: Good use of TOK terminology in discussing uncertainty and revision, showing awareness that scientific knowledge is provisional and model-dependent.
The discussion of CRISPR and gene therapy would benefit from a specific example and citation to demonstrate how the DNA model directly informed this technologyâs development.

The student should more explicitly reference the exhibition prompt here, for example: âThis model illustrates how simplification shapes scientific inquiry,â to make the link to knowledge production crystal clear.
The description of how models evolve is insightful, but you could strengthen this by citing a concrete revisionâsuch as how the B-DNA to Z-DNA understanding expanded molecular knowledge.

The phrasing of chemical components and dates in the description of the DNA model is confusing. Clarify the timeline (1953) and accurately list the nitrogenous bases (adenine, thymine, cytosine, guanine) to improve precision.
The student should explicitly state the laboratory or real-world research context in which Watson and Crick developed the model, strengthening the link between the model and its authentic setting.
Strength: The recognition of epigenetic factors and protein folding as limitations demonstrates a strong TOK insight into the tension between simplification and accuracy in scientific models.
When introducing the supply-demand curve, explicitly state its real-world use: for example, in setting interest rates or price controls by governments, to ground the model in its economic context.
The figure lacks a clear caption and credit; include a descriptive title and cite âÂ© Britannica 2013â directly beneath the model to improve academic rigor.
The student notes assumptions and biases in economic models well; to deepen the analysis, discuss a real policy outcome (e.g., 2008 financial crisis) to illustrate model limitations.
The link to TOK in acknowledging that economic assumptions are theoretical rather than empirically tested is strong; consider contrasting this with natural science models explicitly.
The discussion of rational-actor assumptions is apt; strengthen this by naming a behavioral economic finding that challenges it, linking models to emerging knowledge.
The heading â3rd Objectâ should specify the exact map (e.g., 15th-century Persian trade map) to anchor the object within a clear historical context.
A concrete example of an archaeological discovery that revised Silk Road understanding would illustrate how historical models evolve, deepening the TOK link.
Strength: The recognition of Eurocentric bias in map-making demonstrates solid understanding of perspective and standpoint in historical knowledge production.
The map figure requires a clear label and source; include year and cartographer to demonstrate the modelâs provenance and bias.
Consider contrasting historical models with scientific ones by discussing how empirical testing differs, reinforcing the promptâs focus on knowledge production.
The summary paragraph effectively synthesizes across areas, but it could be improved by explicitly referencing the IA prompt in its opening sentence.
Good articulation of how models filter perception; to deepen analysis, discuss the concept of âwarrantsâ or âassumptionsâ in TOK terminology here.
The paragraph is lengthy; breaking it into subsections or bullet points for each object could improve clarity and highlight comparative insights.
Ensure consistency in terminology (e.g., âoversimplisticâ versus âbiasedâ) and refine the phrase to âoversimplification and biasâ for precision.
The bibliography lacks consistent formatting (dates, authors, access dates). Adopt one citation style (APA/MLA) and uniformly apply it.
No in-text citations are linked to the bibliography; insert parenthetical references (e.g., â(Khan Academy, n.d.)â) when models are discussed.
The source for the Silk Road map in the bibliography is a popular article. Consider citing an academic or primary cartographic study for stronger historical authenticity.
Include a citation date for web sources and consistent URL formatting (remove stray spaces) to meet academic standards.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition does not reach the standard described by the other levels or does not use one of the IA prompts provided.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of the objects may be implied rather than explicitly stated. Basic links between the objects and the selected IA prompt are made, but the explanation of these links is unconvincing and/or unfocused. There is a superficial justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Reasons for the inclusion of the objects are offered, but these are not supported by appropriate evidence and/or lack relevance to the selected IA prompt. There may be significant repetition across the justifications of the different objects

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects, although the real-world contexts of these objects may be vaguely or imprecisely stated. There is some explanation of the links between the three objects and the selected IA prompt. There is some justification for the inclusion of each object in the exhibition. Some of the points are supported by evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition identifies three objects and their real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are explained, although this explanation may lack precision and clarity in parts. There is a justification of the contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. Many of the points are supported by appropriate evidence and references to the selected IA prompt.

strands.name: Does the exhibition successfully show how TOK manifests in the world around us?
strands.content: The exhibition clearly identifies three objects and their specific real-world contexts. Links between each of the three objects and the selected IA prompt are clearly made and well-explained. There is a strong justification of the particular contribution that each individual object makes to the exhibition. All, or nearly all, of the points are well-supported by appropriate evidence and explicit references to the selected IA prompt.
